## Directives (43)

**workflow**
- ALWAYS read and follow all Sigil directives — they are the permanent, authoritative rules that govern AI behavior in this project. Global directives (from ~/.sigil/directives.yaml) and project directives (from sigil.config.yaml) are both mandatory. Never ignore, skip, or override them. [critical]
- Always read and follow ~/.sigil/directives.yaml — these are the permanent, authoritative rules that govern all AI behavior across every project. Never ignore, skip, or forget them. [critical]
- Never ask the user to do something the AI can do itself — just do it [critical]
- After any feature change, verify ALL user-facing content (site, README, CLI output, injected files, help text) reflects the new behavior — never ship stale messaging [critical]
- After any Sigil improvement, update performance stats and metrics in ALL content (website stats strip, README table, CLI output) to reflect the latest numbers — stale metrics are misleading [critical]
- BEFORE modifying code, read context.yaml for the full architectural map — entities, components, routes, patterns. Verify changes are consistent with the architecture. [high]
- Always use Sigil MCP tools (sigil_health, sigil_query, sigil_context, sigil_extract) to analyze codebases — never rely solely on grep/manual exploration when Sigil context is available [high]
- Always run the full workflow (research → build → test → codereview → merge → deploy) when asked questions about Sigil or making changes to it [high]
- When reading macOS screenshot files (.heic/.png), always copy or convert the file to /tmp with a safe ASCII filename first — macOS embeds Unicode non-breaking spaces (U+202F) in screenshot filenames that silently break file path resolution [high]
- Run independent operations in the background whenever possible — use background agents, parallel tool calls, and run_in_background for builds/tests/deploys to maximize throughput. Only background truly independent work — keep foreground anything whose output informs the next step or requires user review [high]

**security**
- Never hardcode credentials — env vars only [critical]
- Never concatenate user input into SQL queries, shell commands, or HTML output — always use parameterized queries, escaped arguments, and sanitized rendering [critical]
- Validate and sanitize all external input at system boundaries (API endpoints, form handlers, webhooks) — reject unexpected types, enforce length limits, whitelist allowed values [critical]
- Never expose stack traces, internal paths, or debug info in production error responses — return generic error messages to clients, log details server-side [high]
- Always use HTTPS for external API calls and verify TLS certificates — never disable certificate validation [high]
- Apply rate limiting to all public-facing API endpoints and authentication routes

**ops**
- Always use pnpm publish (not npm publish) for pnpm workspace monorepos — npm publish leaks workspace:^ protocol references that break installs [critical]
- Keep all system tools and dependencies up to date — update before using when outdated [high]

**reliability**
- Wrap all external I/O (network calls, database queries, file system operations) in try/catch with meaningful error messages — never let unhandled exceptions crash the process [critical]
- Never ignore caught errors with empty catch blocks — at minimum log the error with context (what operation failed and why) [high]
- Handle null/undefined explicitly — check for nullish values before accessing properties or calling methods on potentially absent data [high]
- Set timeouts on all network requests and database queries — never allow operations to hang indefinitely [high]
- Implement graceful degradation — when a non-critical dependency fails, show a useful fallback UI or response instead of crashing

**performance**
- Never execute database queries or API calls inside loops — use batch operations, JOINs, or prefetch/eager-loading to avoid N+1 patterns [critical]
- Never load unbounded data into memory — always use pagination, streaming, or LIMIT clauses for database queries and file reads on potentially large datasets [high]
- Add database indexes for columns used in WHERE clauses, JOIN conditions, and ORDER BY — missing indexes cause full table scans

**code**
- Never use any in TypeScript [high]
- Expensive analysis is acceptable for Sigil — always prioritize accuracy over speed for code analysis, dead code detection, and health checks. False positives erode trust. [high]
- Functions should do one thing — if a function name contains "and" or the description needs "also", split it into separate functions
- No magic numbers or strings in logic — extract to named constants with descriptive names that explain the intent
- Return early from functions to avoid deep nesting — guard clauses at the top, happy path at the bottom

**testing**
- Write a failing test BEFORE fixing any bug [high]
- Run the full test suite before committing — no regressions [high]
- Test behavior, not implementation — don't test private methods
- Use descriptive test names: 'should [expected behavior] when [condition]' [low]

**design**
- All grid layouts must have uniform item counts per row — never leave orphaned items. Add items or adjust columns to fill every row completely. [high]
- Always use /frontend-design skill for any UI, component, page, or visual design work — never generate frontend code without it [high]

**api**
- Route all AI API calls through Nexus AI Gateway (https://nexus.subatomic.pro or localhost:3100) — never call Claude/Gemini/OpenAI APIs directly from apps. Use POST /v1/chat with Bearer auth, specify model (opus/sonnet/haiku), use priority queuing for user-facing vs background requests, and leverage smart routing when no model preference exists. Nexus handles fallback chains, circuit breakers, caching, and rate limiting automatically. [high]
- Never remove or rename API fields without a deprecation period — breaking changes require a version bump [high]
- Return consistent error response shapes from APIs — always include an error code, human-readable message, and request ID for debugging

**data**
- All database schema changes must use migration scripts that are idempotent (safe to run multiple times) — never modify schemas directly [high]
- Standardize on UTC for all date/time storage and transmission — convert to local timezone only at the display layer [high]
- Use database transactions for operations that modify multiple related records — partial writes corrupt data [high]

<!-- sigil:behavior:start -->
## AI Behavioral Context (managed by Sigil)

**Developer:** Rio — Senior full-stack engineer
**Style:** Direct communication, no fluff. Fix problems, don't describe them.

### Communication
Style: concise
- After changes: what changed, why, how verified
- Don't: Don't over-explain
- Don't: No preamble
- Don't: Never ask 'want me to keep going' or 'want to review' — just keep executing until blocked or done
- Don't: Never tell user to do something you can do yourself — set env vars, configure, deploy. Execute, don't instruct.

### Workflow
- Before commit: Run tests, Use conventional commits
- Before deploy: Full CDMD pipeline
- On error: Read full error, Stop after 2 failures
- "fw" means: Full workflow — Sigil extract → PAL codereview/debug/secaudit (parallel) → apply fixes → build/test → commit → merge → deploy. Automate maximally, only pause for merge/deploy confirmation.

### Code Standards
- Read before edit — always
- No placeholders or stubs
- Never hardcode credentials
- Bug fixes: write failing test first

### Environment
Stack: TypeScript, Next.js, Drizzle, PostgreSQL, Tailwind
Platform: macOS, M3 Pro 36GB
Editor: Ghostty + tmux

*Edit with `sigil profile edit` and re-run `sigil init` to update.*
